(also referred to as the **Northeast**, the **East Coast**,[[b]](#cite_note-11) or the **American Northeast**) form one of the four [census regions](/wiki/List_of_regions_of_the_United_States "List of regions of the United States") defined by the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"). Located on the [Atlantic coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") of North America, the region borders [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") to its north, the [Southern United States](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") to its south, the [Midwestern United States](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") to its west, and the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") to its east.

The **Northeastern United States** (also referred to as the **Northeast**, the **East Coast**,[[b]](#cite_note-11) or the **American Northeast**) form one of the four [census regions](/wiki/List_of_regions_of_the_United_States "List of regions of the United States") defined by the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"). Located on the [Atlantic coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") of North America, the region borders [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") to its north, the [Southern United States](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") to its south, the [Midwestern United States](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") to its west, and the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") to its east.

The Northeast is one of the four regions defined by the U.S. Census Bureau for the collection and analysis of statistics.[[1]](#cite_note-Census-1) The Census Bureau defines the region as including the six [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") states of [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut"), [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine"), [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire"), [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island"), and [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont"), and three lower Northeast states of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), and [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"). Some expanded definitions of the region include [Mid-Atlantic](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_(United_States) "Mid-Atlantic (United States)") locations such as [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"), [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland"), [Northern Virginia](/wiki/Northern_Virginia "Northern Virginia"), and [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")[[10]](#cite_note-Hobbs-12)[[11]](#cite_note-Hudson-13)[[12]](#cite_note-McIlwraith-14)[[13]](#cite_note-Shelley-15)[[14]](#cite_note-USDA-16)

The region is the base for the [Northeast megalopolis](/wiki/Northeast_megalopolis "Northeast megalopolis"), which includes many of the nation's largest metropolitan areas, including [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), and [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"). The megalopolis makes up 67% of the region's total population of 57,609,148. The [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") of the region was $5.1 trillion as of 2022 and contains some of the [most developed states](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_Human_Development_Index "List of U.S. states and territories by Human Development Index") based on the [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index"), with every state above the national average.[[15]](#cite_note-:5-17)[[16]](#cite_note-18) It is also the most densely populated region in the United States, with 320 people per square mile (120 people/km2).[[17]](#cite_note-Zelinksy1-19)[[10]](#cite_note-Hobbs-12) The U.S. Census Bureau defines the Northeast United States as having a total area of 181,324 mi2 (469,630 km2), making it the smallest region of the United States by total area.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Indigenous people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Indigenous people")]

Main article: [Indigenous peoples of the Northeastern Woodlands](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Northeastern_Woodlands "Indigenous peoples of the Northeastern Woodlands")

Anthropologists recognize the "Northeastern Woodlands" as one of the [cultural regions](/wiki/Classification_of_indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Classification of indigenous peoples of the Americas") that existed in the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere") at the time of [European colonists](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism") in the 15th and later centuries. Most did not settle in North America until the 17th century. The cultural area, known as the "[Northeastern Woodlands](/wiki/Northeastern_Woodland "Northeastern Woodland")", in addition to covering the entire Northeast U.S., also covered much of what is now Canada and other regions of what is now the [eastern United States](/wiki/Eastern_United_States "Eastern United States").[[18]](#cite_note-20)

Among the many tribes inhabiting this area were those that made up the [Iroquois nations](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") and the numerous [Algonquian peoples](/wiki/Algonquian_peoples "Algonquian peoples").[[19]](#cite_note-Native-21) In the United States of the 21st century, 18 [federally recognized tribes](/wiki/List_of_federally_recognized_tribes_by_state "List of federally recognized tribes by state") reside in the Northeast.[[20]](#cite_note-wildlife-22) For the most part, the people of the Northeastern Woodlands, on whose lands European fishermen began camping to dry their codfish in the early 1600s, lived in villages, especially after being influenced by agricultural traditions of the Ohio and Mississippi valley societies.[[21]](#cite_note-23)

### Colonial history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Colonial history")]

Main article: [History of New England](/wiki/History_of_New_England "History of New England")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Robert_Walter_Weir_-_Embarkation_of_the_Pilgrims_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Robert_Walter_Weir_-_Embarkation_of_the_Pilgrims_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Robert_Walter_Weir_-_Embarkation_of_the_Pilgrims_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*Embarkation of the Pilgrims*, an 1857 portrait by [Robert Walter Weir](/wiki/Robert_Walter_Weir "Robert Walter Weir")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Treaty_of_Penn_with_Indians_by_Benjamin_West.jpg/250px-Treaty_of_Penn_with_Indians_by_Benjamin_West.jpg)](/wiki/File:Treaty_of_Penn_with_Indians_by_Benjamin_West.jpg)

*Penn's Treaty with the Indians*, a 1772 portrait by [Benjamin West](/wiki/Benjamin_West "Benjamin West")

All of the [U.S. states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") making up the Northeastern region were among the original [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies"), though Maine and Vermont were part of other colonies before the United States became independent in the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"). The two cultural and geographic regions that form parts of the Northeastern region have distinct histories. The first European explorer known to have explored the Atlantic shoreline of the Northeast since the [Norse](/wiki/Norse_colonization_of_the_Americas "Norse colonization of the Americas") was [Giovanni da Verrazzano](/wiki/Giovanni_da_Verrazzano "Giovanni da Verrazzano") in 1524. His ship *La Dauphine* explored the coast from what is now known as Florida to New Brunswick.

The first Europeans to settle and colonize New England were [Pilgrims](/wiki/Pilgrims_(Plymouth_Colony) "Pilgrims (Plymouth Colony)") from [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), who landed in present-day Massachusetts in 1620.[[22]](#cite_note-Arenstam-24) The Pilgrims arrived on the ship *[Mayflower](/wiki/Mayflower "Mayflower")* and founded [Plymouth Colony](/wiki/Plymouth_Colony "Plymouth Colony") so they could practice religion freely.[[22]](#cite_note-Arenstam-24) Ten years later, a larger group of [Puritans](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan") settled north of Plymouth Colony in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") to form [Massachusetts Bay Colony](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Colony "Massachusetts Bay Colony").[[23]](#cite_note-25) In 1636, colonists established [Connecticut Colony](/wiki/Connecticut_Colony "Connecticut Colony")[[24]](#cite_note-Kelly_2019-26) and [Providence Plantations](/wiki/Providence_Plantations "Providence Plantations").[[25]](#cite_note-Kelly_2020-27)

Providence was founded by [Roger Williams](/wiki/Roger_Williams "Roger Williams"), who was banished by Massachusetts for his beliefs in freedom of religion, and it was the first colony to guarantee all citizens freedom of worship. [Anne Hutchinson](/wiki/Anne_Hutchinson "Anne Hutchinson"), who was also banished by Massachusetts, formed the town of [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth,_Rhode_Island "Portsmouth, Rhode Island"). Providence, Portsmouth and two other towns ([Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Rhode_Island "Newport, Rhode Island") and [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick,_Rhode_Island "Warwick, Rhode Island")) consolidated to form the [Colony of Rhode Island and Providence Plantations](/wiki/Colony_of_Rhode_Island_and_Providence_Plantations "Colony of Rhode Island and Providence Plantations").[[25]](#cite_note-Kelly_2020-27)

[Henry Hudson](/wiki/Henry_Hudson "Henry Hudson") explored the area of present-day New York in 1609 and claimed it for the Netherlands. His journey stimulated Dutch interest, and the area became known as [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland"). In 1625, the city of [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam") (the location of present-day New York City) was designated the capital of the province.[[26]](#cite_note-28) The Dutch New Netherland settlement along the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") and, for a time, the [New Sweden](/wiki/New_Sweden "New Sweden") settlement along the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") divided the English settlements in the north and the south. In 1664, [Charles II of England](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") formally annexed New Netherland and incorporated it into the [English colonial empire](/wiki/English_colonial_empire "English colonial empire").[[27]](#cite_note-Radford-29) The territory became the colonies of [New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York") and [New Jersey](/wiki/Province_of_New_Jersey "Province of New Jersey").[[27]](#cite_note-Radford-29) New Jersey was originally split into [East Jersey](/wiki/East_Jersey "East Jersey") and [West Jersey](/wiki/West_Jersey "West Jersey") until the two were united as a royal colony in 1702.[[27]](#cite_note-Radford-29)

[New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") played a prominent role in early American education. Starting in the 17th century, the larger towns in New England opened [grammar schools](/wiki/Grammar_schools "Grammar schools"), the forerunner of the modern [high school](/wiki/High_school "High school").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The first public school in the English colonies was the [Boston Latin School](/wiki/Boston_Latin_School "Boston Latin School"), founded in 1635.[[28]](#cite_note-30) In 1636, the colonial [legislature of Massachusetts](/wiki/Legislature_of_Massachusetts "Legislature of Massachusetts") founded [Harvard College](/wiki/Harvard_College "Harvard College"), the oldest [institution of higher learning](/wiki/Higher_education "Higher education") in the United States.[[29]](#cite_note-31)

In 1681, [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn"), who wanted to give [Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers") a land of religious freedom, founded [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Province_of_Pennsylvania "Province of Pennsylvania") and extended freedom of religion to all citizens.[[30]](#cite_note-32)

Penn strongly desired access to the sea for the [Province of Pennsylvania](/wiki/Province_of_Pennsylvania "Province of Pennsylvania") and leased what then came to be known as the "Lower Counties on the Delaware" from the [Duke](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England").[[31]](#cite_note-hod-33) Penn established representative government and briefly combined his two possessions under one General Assembly in 1682.

By 1704, the province of Pennsylvania had grown so large that their representatives wanted to make decisions without the assent of the Lower Counties and the two groups of representatives began meeting on their own, one at [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), and the other at [New Castle, Delaware](/wiki/New_Castle,_Delaware "New Castle, Delaware"). Penn and his heirs remained proprietors of both and always appointed the same person Governor for their province of Pennsylvania and their territory of the Lower Counties. The fact that Delaware and Pennsylvania shared the same governor was not unique. From 1703 to 1738, both New York and New Jersey shared a governor.[[32]](#cite_note-34) Massachusetts and New Hampshire also shared a governor for some time.[[33]](#cite_note-35)

### American Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: American Revolution")]

Further information: [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") and [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Battle_of_Trenton_by_Charles_McBarron.jpg/250px-Battle_of_Trenton_by_Charles_McBarron.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_Trenton_by_Charles_McBarron.jpg)

The [Battle of Trenton](/wiki/Battle_of_Trenton "Battle of Trenton"), fought in New Jersey in December 1776 following [Washington's covert crossing of the Delaware](/wiki/George_Washington%27s_crossing_of_the_Delaware_River "George Washington's crossing of the Delaware River") during the night of December 25, 1776, represented an inspiring victory for Washington's [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army") and began to turn the [Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") in the Americans' favor.

The beginnings of the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") would be in the Northeast, specifically in Massachusetts. The [Battles of Lexington and Concord](/wiki/Battles_of_Lexington_and_Concord "Battles of Lexington and Concord") in northeast of Boston would be the first military engagements between the Revolutionaries and the British.[[34]](#cite_note-Daughan_2018-36)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Many of the major battles of the revolution would be fought in the Northeast. The British would [evacuate Boston](/wiki/Siege_of_Boston "Siege of Boston") in early-1776 and would move to [capture New York City](/wiki/Landing_at_Kip%27s_Bay "Landing at Kip's Bay").[[35]](#cite_note-McCullough_2005-37)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

The revolutionaries were pushed to the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") before suddenly moving forward against the British in the [Battles of Trenton](/wiki/Battle_of_Trenton "Battle of Trenton") and [Princeton](/wiki/Battle_of_Princeton "Battle of Princeton").[[35]](#cite_note-McCullough_2005-37)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] A stalemate was reached in 1778, between the British and American Revolutionaries and continued until the end of the war in 1783.[[36]](#cite_note-38) The war would move to southern states and eventually conclude with the [Battle of Yorktown](/wiki/Siege_of_Yorktown "Siege of Yorktown") in Virginia.[[35]](#cite_note-McCullough_2005-37)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

#### Formation of the United States of America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Formation of the United States of America")]

The idea of an independent United States of America, with the designs of its government would be created primarily in the Northeast in a series of declarations, constitutions, and documents. The [Continental Congresses](/wiki/Continental_Congress "Continental Congress") would meet in Philadelphia, which would produce the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") and [the Articles of Confederation](/wiki/Articles_of_Confederation "Articles of Confederation"). Following the American Revolution, the [capital of the newly formed United States](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States") would move around in the states of [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), and [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"). It was based in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") from 1785 until 1790, when it was moved to [Congress Hall](/wiki/Congress_Hall "Congress Hall") in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), where it remained for a decade, until 1800, when the construction of the new national capital of [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") was completed.[[37]](#cite_note-39)

The [Constitutional Convention](/wiki/Constitutional_Convention_(United_States) "Constitutional Convention (United States)") was held in Philadelphia, where the [new United States Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States") was drafted in 1787.[[38]](#cite_note-40) 6 of the [first 13 states to ratify](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_Constitution#Ratification_of_the_Constitution "History of the United States Constitution") the new constitution would be in the Northeast, with the last of the original 13, Rhode Island, ratifying the constitution in 1790. Vermont would be admitted in 1791 as the 14th state. The [first Congress](/wiki/1st_United_States_Congress "1st United States Congress") would convene in [Federal Hall](/wiki/Federal_Hall "Federal Hall") in New York City in March 1789.[[39]](#cite_note-41)

### Early and mid-19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early and mid-19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Battle_of_Gettysburg%2C_by_Currier_and_Ives.png/250px-Battle_of_Gettysburg%2C_by_Currier_and_Ives.png)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_Gettysburg,_by_Currier_and_Ives.png)

The [Battle of Gettysburg](/wiki/Battle_of_Gettysburg "Battle of Gettysburg"), fought in and around [Gettysburg, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Gettysburg,_Pennsylvania "Gettysburg, Pennsylvania"), was the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War")'s bloodiest battle; the [Union](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army")'s victory at Gettysburg represented a turning point in the war in the Union's favor.

Following the revolution the Northeast would see small skirmishes like the [Whiskey Rebellion](/wiki/Whiskey_Rebellion "Whiskey Rebellion") in western parts of Pennsylvania.[[40]](#cite_note-Berkin_2017-42)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Many northeastern states would continue trading with the British and other European powers. Tensions between the United States and Europe (specifically Britain) would sour in the lead up to the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812").

This caused certain trade merchants to [meet in Hartford](/wiki/Hartford_Convention "Hartford Convention") to propose seceding from the United States.[[41]](#cite_note-43) The [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812") would see less fighting in the Northeast and instead more fighting in western and southern areas. A failed [invasion of Canada](/wiki/Invasion_of_Canada_(1812) "Invasion of Canada (1812)") and the occupation of Maine would be some of the major conflicts during the war.[[42]](#cite_note-44) The war would end in 1815 and most of the Northeast has not seen any major conflict since then.

The [American Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution_in_the_United_States "Industrial Revolution in the United States") was launched in [Blackstone Valley](/wiki/Blackstone_Valley "Blackstone Valley") in [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island") and [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), where [textile mills](/wiki/Textile_manufacturing "Textile manufacturing") spread across New England, and in eastern [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), where coal, steel, and industrialization launched the nation's manufacturing sector.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

After the end of the War of 1812, industry boomed in the Northeast in the early and middle parts of the 19th century. With the construction of railroads and canals crossing the northeast and the rise of western territories and resources from the south, the Northeast experienced the development of new industries and a fast-growing population. Many of the coastal cities, including [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), and [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), served as ocean trade ports for American goods.

Cities, including [Allentown](/wiki/Allentown,_Pennsylvania "Allentown, Pennsylvania"), [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"), [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York"), and [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York"), were settled and emerged as major industrial centers.[[44]](#cite_note-46)

By 1860, New York City, based on its present-day boundaries, was the first U.S. city to reach a population exceeding one million.[[45]](#cite_note-Jackson_1995-47)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Due to the settlement of the [Midwest](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") and [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains"), agriculture would collapse in the Mid-Atlantic and New England, with many farms being abandoned by the end of the century, returning to rural forest.

Conflicts with the south over the [spread of slavery](/wiki/Slave_states_and_free_states "Slave states and free states") would become a large factor in the start of the American Civil War, between the United States (western and Northeastern states) and [the Confederacy](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") (southeastern states). The admission of Maine as a free state in exchange for [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri") becoming a slave state as part of the [Missouri Compromise](/wiki/Missouri_Compromise "Missouri Compromise") in 1820 would settle the final boundaries of the Northeastern states.[[46]](#cite_note-48)

The [Mason-Dixon line](/wiki/Mason%E2%80%93Dixon_line "Mason–Dixon line") would be established as the border of slavery, following the border of Pennsylvania and Delaware/Maryland.[[47]](#cite_note-49) [Abolitionist movements](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States") would start in the Northeast and Midwest and would become prominent towards the mid-19th century, these groups advocated the shrinking or banning of slavery in the United States. Some Northeastern states still had small amounts of slaves into the 1850s, though some would ban it during the decade.

The [election of 1860](/wiki/1860_United_States_presidential_election "1860 United States presidential election") led to the start of the Civil War; southern states seceded from the United States in late-1860 and early-1861. States like [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland") and [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware") would remain in the union, even with slavery still legal. For the first two years, the [eastern theater of the war](/wiki/Eastern_theater_of_the_American_Civil_War "Eastern theater of the American Civil War") would remain in [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") and Maryland, but in 1863 the war would reach its northeastern most extent in [Gettysburg, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Gettysburg,_Pennsylvania "Gettysburg, Pennsylvania"). The [Battle of Gettysburg](/wiki/Battle_of_Gettysburg "Battle of Gettysburg") is considered a turning point in the Civil War, seeing the end of the Confederate push northwards.[[48]](#cite_note-50)

While all Northeastern states would remain in the United States during the war, conflicts did arise, like the [New York draft riots](/wiki/New_York_City_draft_riots "New York City draft riots") in 1863.[[49]](#cite_note-51) The war would end in 1865 with the United States taking back control of Southern states.

### Industrial Revolution and modern times

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Industrial Revolution and modern times")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Mulberry_Street_NYC_c1900_LOC_3g04637u_edit.jpg/250px-Mulberry_Street_NYC_c1900_LOC_3g04637u_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mulberry_Street_NYC_c1900_LOC_3g04637u_edit.jpg)

[Little Italy](/wiki/Little_Italy,_Manhattan "Little Italy, Manhattan") in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan"), c. 1900

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/The_Harmsworth_atlas_and_Gazetter_1908_%28135851517%29.jpg/250px-The_Harmsworth_atlas_and_Gazetter_1908_%28135851517%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Harmsworth_atlas_and_Gazetter_1908_(135851517).jpg)

Northeastern United States in 1908 from *The Harmsworth atlas and Gazetter*

Following the Civil War, the Northeast would see a large economic boom and would become one of the most industrialized regions in the world. Many technological innovations would be made in the Northeast during this time. The [Second Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Second_Industrial_Revolution "Second Industrial Revolution") would see the northeast grow massively, even more so than before the Civil War. Many cities in the Northeast would explode in population, with cities like Philadelphia and New York climbing over one million people, while other cities like Buffalo, Boston, and Pittsburgh would rise above half a million during this time.

New York City eventually grew to become one of the largest cities in the world by 1900. With the American involvement in both World Wars, the Northeast would become a large base of war production, with the [Brooklyn Naval Yard](/wiki/Brooklyn_Navy_Yard "Brooklyn Navy Yard") producing many navy ships.[[50]](#cite_note-52) Many worker strikes would occur in the states, including the [Homestead strike](/wiki/Homestead_strike "Homestead strike") in 1892.[[51]](#cite_note-53) Many of these cities would see a peak population and industrial output in the aftermath of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") in the 1950s.[[52]](#cite_note-54)

Starting in the 1950s and continuing into the 21st century, a large [industrial decline](/wiki/Deindustrialization "Deindustrialization") in the Northeast resulted in a depopulation of many Northeastern cities, many of which had not yet recovered from it into the 21st century. This led to the rise of programs of [urban renewal](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal") and demolition of large parts of Northeastern cities during the mid and late 20th century.[[53]](#cite_note-Sugrue_2014-55)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] There has also been a large population shift to the [Sun Belt states](/wiki/Sun_Belt "Sun Belt") starting in the 1960s.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

[New York state](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") lost its claim to being the most populated state after it was surpassed by [California](/wiki/California "California") in the 1970s. Some Northeastern cities, including New York City, have recovered from their decline in the mid-20th century.[[45]](#cite_note-Jackson_1995-47)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Many new information and service industries have risen in the northeast, which has led to a boom in the 21st century in some cities in the Northeast like Boston, New York, and Philadelphia. Some other cities like [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut"), Syracuse, and Buffalo still are declining though in the 21st century.[[55]](#cite_note-57) [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy") would impact much of the northeast in 2012, severely damaging much of the coast and causing flooding inland. The hurricane would directly impact [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") and caused large amounts of flooding in New York City.[[56]](#cite_note-58)

Although the first settlers of New England were motivated by religion, since the 21st century, [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") had become one of the least religious parts of the United States. In a 2009 [Gallup](/wiki/The_Gallup_Organization "The Gallup Organization") survey, less than half of residents in [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine"), [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire"), and [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont") reported religion as an important part of their daily life.[[57]](#cite_note-59) In a 2010 Gallup survey, less than 30% of residents in Vermont, New Hampshire, Maine, and Massachusetts reported [attending church](/wiki/Church_attendance "Church attendance") weekly, giving them the lowest church attendance among U.S. states.[[58]](#cite_note-60)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

The vast area from central [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") to northern [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine"), and from western [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), from [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh") in the west to the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") in the east, have all been loosely grouped into the Northeast at one time or another.
The [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau")'s definition of the Northeast includes nine states: [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont"), [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island"), [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut"), [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire"), and [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania").[[1]](#cite_note-Census-1)[[c]](#cite_note-62)

The region is often subdivided into [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England"), the six states east of New York state and the [Mid-Atlantic states](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_(United_States) "Mid-Atlantic (United States)") of New Jersey, New York, Pennsylvania and areas to the south. This definition has been essentially unchanged since 1880 and is widely used as a standard for data tabulation.[[60]](#cite_note-63)[[61]](#cite_note-widely-64)[[62]](#cite_note-65)[[63]](#cite_note-66)

The U.S. Census Bureau has acknowledged the obvious limitations of this definition and the potential merits of a proposal created after the 1950 census,[[64]](#cite_note-67) that would include changing regional boundaries to include [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"), [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland"), and [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") with the Mid-Atlantic states, but ultimately decided that "the new system did not win enough overall acceptance among data users to warrant adoption as an official new set of general-purpose State groupings. The previous development of many series of statistics, arranged and issued over long periods of time on the basis of the existing State groupings, favored the retention of the summary units of the current regions and divisions."[[65]](#cite_note-GARM-68) The U.S. Census Bureau confirmed in 1994 that it would continue to "review the components of the regions and divisions to ensure that they continue to represent the most useful combinations of states and state equivalents."[[65]](#cite_note-GARM-68)

Many organizations and reference works follow the Census Bureau's definition for the region.[[66]](#cite_note-69)[[67]](#cite_note-70)[[68]](#cite_note-71) In the history of the United States, the [Mason–Dixon line](/wiki/Mason%E2%80%93Dixon_line "Mason–Dixon line") between Pennsylvania ([the North](/wiki/Northern_United_States "Northern United States")) and Maryland ([the South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States")) traditionally divided the regions,[[69]](#cite_note-72) but in modern times, various entities define the Northeastern United States in somewhat different ways.

The [Association of American Geographers](/wiki/Association_of_American_Geographers "Association of American Geographers") divides the Northeast into two divisions: "New England", which is the same as the Census Bureau; and it has the same "Middle States" but adds Delaware.[[70]](#cite_note-73) Similarly, the [Geological Society of America](/wiki/Geological_Society_of_America "Geological Society of America") defines the Northeast as these same states but with the addition of Maryland and the [District of Columbia](/wiki/District_of_Columbia "District of Columbia").[[71]](#cite_note-74)

The narrowest definitions include only the states of New England.[[72]](#cite_note-75) Other more restrictive definitions include New England and New York as part of the Northeast United States, but exclude Pennsylvania and New Jersey.[[73]](#cite_note-76)[[74]](#cite_note-77)

States beyond the Census Bureau definition are included in Northeast Region by various other entities:

* Various organizations include [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"), [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland"), and the national capital of [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")[[10]](#cite_note-Hobbs-12)[[11]](#cite_note-Hudson-13)[[12]](#cite_note-McIlwraith-14)[[13]](#cite_note-Shelley-15)[[75]](#cite_note-NRC-78)[[14]](#cite_note-USDA-16)
* The [U.S. Environmental Protection Agency](/wiki/United_States_Environmental_Protection_Agency "United States Environmental Protection Agency") and [National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration](/wiki/National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration "National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration") include in their Northeast Region Delaware, Maryland, and [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia").[[76]](#cite_note-79)[[77]](#cite_note-80)[[78]](#cite_note-81)
* The [United States Fish and Wildlife Service](/wiki/United_States_Fish_and_Wildlife_Service "United States Fish and Wildlife Service") includes in their Northeast Region: Delaware, Maryland, District of Columbia, West Virginia, and [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia").[[79]](#cite_note-82)
* The [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") includes in their Northeast Region: Delaware, Maryland, West Virginia, and Virginia, though small parts are also in the National Capital Region.[[80]](#cite_note-83)

### Topography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Topography")]

While most of the Northeastern United States lie in the physiographic region of the [Appalachian Highlands](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains"), some are also part of the [Atlantic coastal plain](/wiki/Atlantic_coastal_plain "Atlantic coastal plain"), which extends south to the southern tip of [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida"). The coastal plain areas include [Cape Cod](/wiki/Cape_Cod "Cape Cod") in Massachusetts, [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") in New York, and most of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), and are generally low and flat with sandy soil and long tidal marsh waterways.[[10]](#cite_note-Hobbs-12) The highlands, including the [Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont_(United_States) "Piedmont (United States)") and the Appalachian Mountains, are heavily forested, ranging from rolling hills to summits greater than 6,000 feet (1,800 m), and pocked with many lakes.[[10]](#cite_note-Hobbs-12) The highest peak in the Northeast is [Mount Washington](/wiki/Mount_Washington_(New_Hampshire) "Mount Washington (New Hampshire)") in [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire") at 6,288 feet (1,917 m).[[81]](#cite_note-84)

### Land use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Land use")]

As of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit), forest-use covered approximately 60% of the Northeastern states, including Delaware, Maryland, and Washington, D.C., about twice the national average. About 11% was cropland and another 4% grassland pasture or range. There is also more urbanized land in the Northeast (12%) than in any other U.S region.[[14]](#cite_note-USDA-16)

Many parks on a state and national level cover the inland parts of the region. Large parks include the [Adirondack Park](/wiki/Adirondack_Park "Adirondack Park") in northeastern New York, [Green Mountain National Forest](/wiki/Green_Mountain_National_Forest "Green Mountain National Forest") in Vermont, [White Mountain Forest](/wiki/White_Mountain_National_Forest "White Mountain National Forest") in northern New Hampshire, [Baxter State Park](/wiki/Baxter_State_Park "Baxter State Park") in northern Maine, [Acadia National Park](/wiki/Acadia_National_Park "Acadia National Park") on the eastern coast of Maine, [Allegheny National Forest](/wiki/Allegheny_National_Forest "Allegheny National Forest") in northwestern Pennsylvania, and [Catskill Park](/wiki/Catskill_Park "Catskill Park") in southern New York. There are also some parks closer to the shore, though these are usually smaller and squeezed in-between urbanized areas. These include the [Palisades Park](/wiki/The_Palisades_(Hudson_River) "The Palisades (Hudson River)") in New Jersey, [Fire Island](/wiki/Fire_Island_National_Seashore "Fire Island National Seashore") in Long Island, and the [Cape Cod shoreline](/wiki/Cape_Cod_National_Seashore "Cape Cod National Seashore") in Massachusetts. The Northeast has 72 [National Wildlife Refuges](/wiki/National_Wildlife_Refuge "National Wildlife Refuge"), encompassing more than 500,000 acres (780 sq mi; 2,000 km2) of habitat and designed to protect some of the 92 different threatened and [endangered species](/wiki/Endangered_species "Endangered species") living in the region.[[20]](#cite_note-wildlife-22)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Climate")]

The climate of the Northeastern United States varies from the northernmost state of [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine") to its southernmost state in [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland"). The region's climate is influenced by its positional western to eastern flow of weather in the lower middle latitudes in the United States. In summer the subtropical high ([Bermuda High](/wiki/Bermuda_High "Bermuda High")) moves toward the East Coast, this pumps warm and sultry air toward the Northeast (less so in the far northern areas of northern New York, Vermont, New Hampshire, and Maine). Summers are normally warm in northern areas to hot in southern areas. Frequent (but brief) thundershowers are common on hot summer days from Connecticut south to Maryland.

In winter, the subtropical high retreats southeastward, and the polar jet stream moves south bringing colder air masses from up in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") and more frequent storm systems to the region. Winter often brings both rain and snow as well as surges of both warm and cold air. In the southern part of the Northeast from coastal Rhode Island southwest to eastern Maryland, the Appalachians partially protect these locations from the extreme cold coming from the west and the interior of North America.[[82]](#cite_note-Climate-85)

The basic climate of the Northeast can be divided into a colder and snowier interior, including western Maryland, most of Pennsylvania, most of [North Jersey](/wiki/North_Jersey "North Jersey"), [Upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York"), and most of New England, and a milder coastal plain region from [Cape Cod](/wiki/Cape_Cod "Cape Cod") and southern [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island") southward, including [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), [Southern Connecticut](/wiki/Southern_Connecticut "Southern Connecticut"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), central and southern [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), part of the Pennsylvania portion of the [Delaware Valley](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley") including [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"), and most of [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland").

In the latter region the [hardiness zone](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone") ranges from 7a to 8a. Annual mean temperatures range from the low-to-mid 50s F from Maryland to southern Connecticut, to the 40s F in most of New York State, New England, and northern Pennsylvania.[[82]](#cite_note-Climate-85)[[83]](#cite_note-86)[[84]](#cite_note-87)

Most of the Northeast has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") (*Dfa/Dfb/occasional Dfc/Dc*). The northernmost portion of the [humid subtropical](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") zone (*Cfa/Do*) begins at [Martha's Vineyard](/wiki/Martha%27s_Vineyard "Martha's Vineyard") and [extreme SW Rhode Island](/wiki/Westerly,_Rhode_Island "Westerly, Rhode Island") and extends southwestward down the coastal plain to central and southern Maryland. The [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") zone (*Cfb/Do*) only exists on [Block Island](/wiki/Block_Island "Block Island") and [Nantucket](/wiki/Nantucket "Nantucket"). It is the only area of the Northeast where all months average between 0 and 22 °C (32 and 72 °F). [Cape Cod](/wiki/Cape_Cod "Cape Cod") borders this zone and warm-summer humid continental (*Dfb/Dc*).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Ethnic origins in the Northeast

As of the [2020 U.S. census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the population of the region was 57,609,148, representing 17.38% of the nation's total population.[[5]](#cite_note-auto-6) With an average of 345.5 people per square mile, the Northeast is 2.5 times as densely populated as the second-most dense region, the [South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"). Since the last century, the [U.S. population has been shifting away](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_population_growth_rate "List of U.S. states by population growth rate") from the Northeast and [Midwest](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") toward the [South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") and [West](/wiki/Western_United_States "Western United States").[[87]](#cite_note-90)

The region's racial composition as of 2020 was 64.42% [white](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States_census "Race and ethnicity in the United States census"), 11.51% African American, 0.51% Native American, 7.25% Asian, 0.04% Pacific Islander, 8.17% from other races, and 8.10% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") of any race were 15.27% of the population.[[88]](#cite_note-91) There were 22,418,883 households and 14,189,719 families in 2021. Of the 22,418,883 households, 27.7% included children under the age of 18.[[89]](#cite_note-92)

In 2021, the region's the population's age distribution was 20.5% under age 18, 57.36% from 18 to 62, and 22.1% who were 62 years of age or older. The median age was 40.5 years. For every 100 females, there were 96.4 males. For every 100 women ages 18 and over, there were 94.3 men.[[90]](#cite_note-93)

The median income for a household in the region in 2021 was $77,142, and the median income for a family was $97,347. About 11.9% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 16.0% of those under age 18 and 10.4% of those age 65 or over.[[91]](#cite_note-94)

The two U.S. Census Bureau divisions in the Northeast, [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") and the [Mid-Atlantic](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_(United_States) "Mid-Atlantic (United States)"), rank second and first respectively among the [9 divisions in population density](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_population_density "List of U.S. states by population density") according to the 2013 population estimate. The [South Atlantic](/wiki/South_Atlantic_states "South Atlantic states") region (233.1) was very close behind New England (233.2). Due to the faster growth of the [South Atlantic](/wiki/South_Atlantic_states "South Atlantic states") region, it will take over the #2 division rank in population density in the next estimate, dropping New England to 3rd position. New England is projected to retain the number 3 rank for many, many years, as the only other lower-ranked division with even half the population density of New England is the [East North Central](/wiki/East_North_Central_states "East North Central states") division (192.1) and this region's population is projected to grow slowly.[[d]](#cite_note-96)[[92]](#cite_note-census.gov-95)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Economy")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=). *(February 2023)* |

As of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit), the Northeast U.S. accounts for approximately 23% of the nation's [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product").[[15]](#cite_note-:5-17) Due to its vast population and diverse landscapes, the Northeast has a large and robust economy, ranging from financial services in Manhattan, to agriculture in [Central Pennsylvania](/wiki/South_Central_Pennsylvania "South Central Pennsylvania").

### New York City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: New York City")]

Main article: [Economy of New York City](/wiki/Economy_of_New_York_City "Economy of New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Photos_NewYork1_032.jpg/250px-Photos_NewYork1_032.jpg)](/wiki/File:Photos_NewYork1_032.jpg)

The [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange_Building "New York Stock Exchange Building") in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") is the largest stock exchange in the world by market capitalization.

As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit), the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") is estimated to produce a [gross metropolitan product](/wiki/Gross_metropolitan_product "Gross metropolitan product") (GMP) of $2.1 trillion US dollars, ranking it [first in the U.S.](/wiki/List_of_U.S._metropolitan_areas_by_GDP "List of U.S. metropolitan areas by GDP") If the New York metropolitan area were a [sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state"), it would have been the [eighth-largest economy](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)") in the world. [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") is considered the world's financial center, with many large banks based in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") and some of the largest stock exchanges on [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street"), like the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange"), it is so prominent that the term "Wall Street" is usually synonymous with finance. Many other companies are based in New York City area, either in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"), [downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn"), [Long Island City](/wiki/Long_Island_City "Long Island City"), or the various suburbs, like [Stamford](/wiki/Stamford,_Connecticut "Stamford, Connecticut") or [White Plains](/wiki/White_Plains,_New_York "White Plains, New York"). Some of the largest companies based in New York City area include, [Verizon](/wiki/Verizon_Communications "Verizon Communications"), [J.P. Morgan Chase](/wiki/JPMorgan_Chase "JPMorgan Chase"), [Citigroup](/wiki/Citigroup "Citigroup"), [MetLife](/wiki/MetLife "MetLife"), [PepsiCo](/wiki/PepsiCo "PepsiCo"), [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM"), [Time Warner](/wiki/WarnerMedia "WarnerMedia"), [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs"), and [Pfizer](/wiki/Pfizer "Pfizer"). Several technology companies have been founded in New York, or moved their headquarters to New York from other places.

[New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") is the nation's most populated city, and the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") including and surrounding it is the nation's most populated metropolitan region, contributing to a sizable shopping economy, including many large shopping malls and department stores based in the area, such as [Macy's on 34th Street](/wiki/Macy%27s_Herald_Square "Macy's Herald Square"), [Fifth Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue "Fifth Avenue"), and [American Dream](/wiki/American_Dream_Meadowlands "American Dream Meadowlands") in [East Rutherford, New Jersey](/wiki/East_Rutherford,_New_Jersey "East Rutherford, New Jersey"), the [Palisades Center](/wiki/Palisades_Center "Palisades Center") in [West Nyack, New York](/wiki/West_Nyack,_New_York "West Nyack, New York"), and the [SoNo Collection](/wiki/The_SoNo_Collection "The SoNo Collection") in [Norwalk, Connecticut](/wiki/Norwalk,_Connecticut "Norwalk, Connecticut"). The [Port of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey "Port of New York and New Jersey"), one of the nation's largest ports, is located on [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor").

As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit), the [Philadelphia metropolitan area](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley") is estimated to produce a GMP of $479 billion US dollars, making it the 9th largest economy in the United States. Many large companies are based in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), the nation's sixth-largest city, including [AmerisourceBergen](/wiki/AmerisourceBergen "AmerisourceBergen"), [Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast"), and [DuPont](/wiki/DuPont "DuPont"). ### Boston

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Boston")]

The [Boston metropolitan area](/wiki/Greater_Boston "Greater Boston") is a major center for insurance, finance, and technology, serving as the global headquarters for [General Electric](/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric"), [Liberty Mutual](/wiki/Liberty_Mutual "Liberty Mutual"), and other large companies.

### Other regions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Other regions")]

Rural regions and states, including most of [Upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York"), [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont"), [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire"), and [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine"), rely more on agriculture, logging, mining, and tourism to help boost their local and statewide economies. Many national and state parks in the region generate lots of tourism, especially during fall months. The logging industry is especially prominent in Maine, making up a large part of [Northern Maine](/wiki/North_Maine_Woods "North Maine Woods")'s economy.

Many Northeastern states have very large economies and are highly developed. As of 2022, the per capita [gross domestic products](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") for these states are:

1. **[New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)")**, US$2.1 trillion, per capita $105,226
2. **[Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania")**, US$931 billion, per capita $71,825
3. **[New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey")**, US$753 billion, per capita $81,307
4. **[Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts")**, US$693 billion, per capita $99,274
5. **[Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut")**, US$323 billion, per capita $89,301
6. **[New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire")**, US$106 billion, per capita $76,008
7. **[Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine")**, US$85 billion, per capita $61,491
8. **[Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island")**, US$72 billion per capita $65,879
9. **[Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont")**, US$41 billion, per capita $63,275

The [Nuclear Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Nuclear_Regulatory_Commission "Nuclear Regulatory Commission") oversees 34 nuclear reactors, eight for research or testing and 26 for [power production](/wiki/Nuclear_power "Nuclear power") in the Northeastern United States.[[75]](#cite_note-NRC-78)

#### Major stations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Major stations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Image-Grand_central_Station_Outside_Night_2.jpg/250px-Image-Grand_central_Station_Outside_Night_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Image-Grand_central_Station_Outside_Night_2.jpg)

[Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal") in New York City, a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") and the second-busiest train station in the nation after [New York Penn Station](/wiki/New_York_Penn_Station "New York Penn Station"), also in New York City

* [30th Street Station](/wiki/30th_Street_Station "30th Street Station") in Philadelphia. Served by all SEPTA Regional Lines, Amtrak, NJ Transit's [Atlantic City Line](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Line "Atlantic City Line"), it is the third-busiest [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") station and 11th-busiest train station in North America with over four million passengers in 2019.
* [Pennsylvania Station](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Station_(New_York_City) "Pennsylvania Station (New York City)") in New York City is served by some [NJ Transit](/wiki/NJ_Transit "NJ Transit") lines, and some [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road") and [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") trains. It is the [busiest train station in North America](/wiki/List_of_busiest_railway_stations_in_North_America "List of busiest railway stations in North America"), with over 10 million passengers in 2019, along with 27 million passengers from NJ Transit and 69 million from Long Island Rail in 2017.
* [Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal") in New York City is served by Metro North and some Long Island Rail trains (beginning in January 2023). Grand Central Terminal had over 67 million annual passengers in 2017 and is the second-busiest train station in the nation and third-busiest in North America.
* [Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(New_Haven) "Union Station (New Haven)") in [New Haven, Connecticut](/wiki/New_Haven,_Connecticut "New Haven, Connecticut") is served by [New Haven Line](/wiki/New_Haven_Line "New Haven Line"), Hartford Line, and Shoreline East along existing [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") train lines. It had 350,000 annual Amtrak passengers in 2017.
* [South Station](/wiki/South_Station "South Station") in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") is served by southern MBTA commuter lines and Amtrak, and was the seventh-busiest train station in North America with nearly 29 million passengers as of 2017.
* [North Station](/wiki/North_Station "North Station") in Boston is served by northern MBTA commuter lines and the [Downeaster](/wiki/Downeaster_(train) "Downeaster (train)") on Amtrak. It had six million MBTA users in 2012 and 152,000 Amtrak passengers in 2021.

The [New Jersey Turnpike](/wiki/New_Jersey_Turnpike "New Jersey Turnpike") (I-95) is one of the busiest highways in the nation.

Many other minor highways exist in the Northeast, connecting cities. Major US Routes which run through the Northeast include [US 1](/wiki/U.S._Route_1 "U.S. Route 1"), [US 2](/wiki/U.S._Route_2 "U.S. Route 2"), [US 3](/wiki/U.S._Route_3 "U.S. Route 3"), [US 4](/wiki/U.S._Route_4 "U.S. Route 4"), [US 5](/wiki/U.S._Route_5 "U.S. Route 5"), [US 6](/wiki/U.S._Route_6 "U.S. Route 6"), [US 7](/wiki/U.S._Route_7 "U.S. Route 7"), [US 9](/wiki/U.S._Route_9 "U.S. Route 9"), [US 11](/wiki/U.S._Route_11 "U.S. Route 11"), [US 13](/wiki/U.S._Route_13 "U.S. Route 13"), [US 15](/wiki/U.S._Route_15 "U.S. Route 15"), [US 19](/wiki/U.S._Route_19 "U.S. Route 19"), [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20 "U.S. Route 20"), [US 22](/wiki/U.S._Route_22 "U.S. Route 22"), [US 29](/wiki/U.S._Route_29 "U.S. Route 29"), [US 30](/wiki/U.S._Route_30 "U.S. Route 30"), [US 40](/wiki/U.S._Route_40 "U.S. Route 40"), [US 44](/wiki/U.S._Route_44 "U.S. Route 44"), [US 46](/wiki/U.S._Route_46 "U.S. Route 46"), [US 50](/wiki/U.S._Route_50 "U.S. Route 50"), [US 62](/wiki/U.S._Route_62 "U.S. Route 62"), [US 113](/wiki/U.S._Route_113 "U.S. Route 113"), [US 130](/wiki/U.S._Route_130 "U.S. Route 130"), [US 201](/wiki/U.S._Route_201 "U.S. Route 201"), [US 202](/wiki/U.S._Route_202 "U.S. Route 202"), [US 206](/wiki/U.S._Route_206 "U.S. Route 206"), [US 209](/wiki/U.S._Route_209 "U.S. Route 209"), [US 219](/wiki/U.S._Route_219 "U.S. Route 219"), [US 220](/wiki/U.S._Route_220 "U.S. Route 220"), [US 222](/wiki/U.S._Route_222 "U.S. Route 222"), [US 224](/wiki/U.S._Route_224 "U.S. Route 224"), [US 301](/wiki/U.S._Route_301 "U.S. Route 301"), [US 302](/wiki/U.S._Route_302 "U.S. Route 302"), [US 322](/wiki/U.S._Route_322 "U.S. Route 322"), [US 340](/wiki/U.S._Route_340 "U.S. Route 340"), [US 422](/wiki/U.S._Route_422 "U.S. Route 422"), [US 522](/wiki/U.S._Route_522 "U.S. Route 522").

The Northeast has the highest amount of tolled roads/bridges in the nation with only two states in the Northeast having no tolls, Connecticut and Vermont. Notable turnpikes include the [Pennsylvania Turnpike](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Turnpike "Pennsylvania Turnpike") (I-76/I-276/I-95), [New Jersey Turnpike](/wiki/New_Jersey_Turnpike "New Jersey Turnpike") (partially I-95), [New York Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") (I-87/I-90), [Massachusetts Turnpike](/wiki/Massachusetts_Turnpike "Massachusetts Turnpike") (I-90), [Maine Turnpike](/wiki/Interstate_95_in_Maine "Interstate 95 in Maine") (I-95), [PA Turnpike Northeast Extension](/wiki/Interstate_476 "Interstate 476") (I-476). The northeast also contains many tolled and non-tolled parkways, many of which are in New York City metro. Major parkways include the [Garden State Parkway](/wiki/Garden_State_Parkway "Garden State Parkway"), [Taconic State Parkway](/wiki/Taconic_State_Parkway "Taconic State Parkway"), [Hutchinson River Parkway](/wiki/Hutchinson_River_Parkway "Hutchinson River Parkway"), [Saw Mill River Parkway](/wiki/Saw_Mill_River_Parkway "Saw Mill River Parkway"), [Lake Ontario State Parkway](/wiki/Lake_Ontario_State_Parkway "Lake Ontario State Parkway"), [Niagara Scenic Parkway](/wiki/Niagara_Scenic_Parkway "Niagara Scenic Parkway"), [Belt Parkway](/wiki/Belt_Parkway "Belt Parkway"), [Grand Central Parkway](/wiki/Grand_Central_Parkway "Grand Central Parkway"), [Northern State Parkway](/wiki/Northern_State_Parkway "Northern State Parkway").

#### Major crossings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Major crossings")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/George_Washington_Bridge_001.JPG/330px-George_Washington_Bridge_001.JPG)](/wiki/File:George_Washington_Bridge_001.JPG)

The [George Washington Bridge](/wiki/George_Washington_Bridge "George Washington Bridge") crossing the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"), carrying most traffic on Interstate 95 from New Jersey to New York.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Driscoll-Viesser-Edison_Bridges_-_August_30%2C_2020.jpg/330px-Driscoll-Viesser-Edison_Bridges_-_August_30%2C_2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Driscoll-Viesser-Edison_Bridges_-_August_30,_2020.jpg)

The [Driscoll Bridge](/wiki/Driscoll_Bridge "Driscoll Bridge") is one of the world's widest and busiest motor vehicle bridges, crossing the [Raritan River](/wiki/Raritan_River "Raritan River") on New Jersey's [Garden State Parkway](/wiki/Garden_State_Parkway "Garden State Parkway").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Holland_Tunnel_Eastbound.jpg/330px-Holland_Tunnel_Eastbound.jpg)](/wiki/File:Holland_Tunnel_Eastbound.jpg)

The [Holland Tunnel](/wiki/Holland_Tunnel "Holland Tunnel") crossing under the Hudson River, connecting [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey") with [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan").

* [Delaware Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Delaware_Memorial_Bridge "Delaware Memorial Bridge") ([I-295](/wiki/Interstate_295_(Delaware%E2%80%93Pennsylvania) "Interstate 295 (Delaware–Pennsylvania)"), [NJ Turnpike](/wiki/New_Jersey_Turnpike "New Jersey Turnpike")) - Crosses the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") between Delaware and New Jersey, the southern most fixed crossing on the river. Eastbound span opened in 1951, westbound span opened in 1968.
* [Walt Whitman Bridge](/wiki/Walt_Whitman_Bridge "Walt Whitman Bridge") (I-76) - Crosses the Delaware River, connecting southern Philadelphia to [southern New Jersey suburbs](/wiki/South_Jersey "South Jersey"). Opened in 1957.
* [Benjamín Franklin Bridge](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin_Bridge "Benjamin Franklin Bridge") ([I-676](/wiki/Interstate_676 "Interstate 676")) - Connects [downtown Philadelphia](/wiki/Center_City,_Philadelphia "Center City, Philadelphia") with [Camden, New Jersey](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey") and southern New Jersey. Opened in 1926.
* [Delaware River Turnpike Toll Bridge](/wiki/Delaware_River%E2%80%93Turnpike_Toll_Bridge "Delaware River–Turnpike Toll Bridge") (I-95, [PA Turnpike](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Turnpike "Pennsylvania Turnpike")) - Connects the PA Turnpike to the NJ Turnpike. I-95 was only designated in 2018, formerly was I-276. Opened in 1956.
* [Driscoll Bridge](/wiki/Driscoll_Bridge "Driscoll Bridge") ([Garden State Parkway](/wiki/Garden_State_Parkway "Garden State Parkway")) - Crosses the [Raritan River](/wiki/Raritan_River "Raritan River") near its mouth at [Raritan Bay](/wiki/Raritan_Bay "Raritan Bay"). Opened in 1954, the bridge is located within the [heart of the state of New Jersey](/wiki/Central_Jersey "Central Jersey"), providing access to the [Jersey Shore](/wiki/Jersey_Shore "Jersey Shore") to the south and [North Jersey](/wiki/North_Jersey "North Jersey") to the north.
* [Newark Bay Bridge](/wiki/Newark_Bay_Bridge "Newark Bay Bridge") (I-76, NJ Turnpike) - Crosses Newark Bay, connecting Newark to Jersey City and [Bayonne](/wiki/Bayonne,_New_Jersey "Bayonne, New Jersey"). Opened in 1956.
* [Bayonne Bridge](/wiki/Bayonne_Bridge "Bayonne Bridge") ([NJ 440](/wiki/New_Jersey_Route_440 "New Jersey Route 440")/[NY 440](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_440 "New York State Route 440")) - Crosses [Arthur Kill](/wiki/Arthur_Kill "Arthur Kill"), connecting Bayonne to [Staten Island, New York](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island"). Opened in 1931, raised in 2017.
* [Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge](/wiki/Verrazzano%E2%80%93Narrows_Bridge "Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge") ([I-278](/wiki/Interstate_278 "Interstate 278")) - Crosses [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor") to connect Staten Island to [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), double decked. Opened in 1964.
* [Brooklyn-Battery Tunnel](/wiki/Brooklyn%E2%80%93Battery_Tunnel "Brooklyn–Battery Tunnel") (I-478) - Crosses underneath the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") to connect [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") to Brooklyn. Opened in 1950.
* [Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge"), [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge"), [Williamsburg Bridge](/wiki/Williamsburg_Bridge "Williamsburg Bridge") - The bridges cross the East River to connect Lower Manhattan to Brooklyn. Opened in 1883, 1909, and 1903.
* [PATH Hudson Tubes](/wiki/PATH_(rail_system)#Construction "PATH (rail system)") - Carries the PATH metro trains underneath the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") to connect Newark, Jersey City, and Hoboken to Lower and [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"). The tubes opened up in 1908/1909.
* [Holland Tunnel](/wiki/Holland_Tunnel "Holland Tunnel") (I-78) - Crosses underneath the Hudson River, connects road traffic from Jersey City and Newark to Lower Manhattan. Opened in 1927.
* [Queens-Midtown Tunnel](/wiki/Queens%E2%80%93Midtown_Tunnel "Queens–Midtown Tunnel") ([I-495](/wiki/Interstate_495_(New_York) "Interstate 495 (New York)")) - Crosses underneath the East River, connecting the Long Island Expressway to Manhattan. Opened in 1940.
* [North River Tunnels](/wiki/North_River_Tunnels "North River Tunnels") (NJ Transit, Amtrak) Carries the [Northeast Corridor](/wiki/Northeast_Corridor "Northeast Corridor") underneath the Hudson River, connecting rail traffic from New Jersey to [Pennsylvania Station](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Station_(New_York_City) "Pennsylvania Station (New York City)") and New England. Opened in 1910.
* [Lincoln Tunnel](/wiki/Lincoln_Tunnel "Lincoln Tunnel") ([NJ 495](/wiki/New_Jersey_Route_495 "New Jersey Route 495")) - Crosses underneath the Hudson River to connect New Jersey to Midtown Manhattan. Opened in 1937.
* [Queensboro Bridge](/wiki/Queensboro_Bridge "Queensboro Bridge") ([NY 25](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_25 "New York State Route 25")) - Connects Midtown Manhattan to Long Island City. Opened in 1909.
* [Triborough Bridge](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy_Bridge "Robert F. Kennedy Bridge") (I-278) - Crosses the East River and [Harlem River](/wiki/Harlem_River "Harlem River"), provides road connections to [Upper Manhattan](/wiki/Upper_Manhattan "Upper Manhattan"), Queens, and [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx"). Also crosses and connects [Randalls Island](/wiki/Randalls_and_Wards_Islands "Randalls and Wards Islands"). Opened in 1936.
* [Hell Gate Bridge](/wiki/Hell_Gate_Bridge "Hell Gate Bridge") (Amtrak) - Crosses the East River and [Bronx Kill](/wiki/Bronx_Kill "Bronx Kill"), provides a rail connection from Pennsylvania Station to the Bronx and Connecticut. Opened in 1917.

### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Central_Railroad_of_New_Jersey_Terminal.jpg/250px-Central_Railroad_of_New_Jersey_Terminal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Central_Railroad_of_New_Jersey_Terminal.jpg)

The [Jersey City Terminal](/wiki/Central_Railroad_of_New_Jersey_Terminal "Central Railroad of New Jersey Terminal"), a major rail and ferry connection between New Jersey and New York City.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/CBX_Parkchester_6_jeh.JPG/250px-CBX_Parkchester_6_jeh.JPG)](/wiki/File:CBX_Parkchester_6_jeh.JPG)

The Cross-Bronx Expressway (I-95) is an urban freeway which was built using [slum clearance](/wiki/Slum_clearance_in_the_United_States "Slum clearance in the United States") policies in the 1950s and 1960s. Today it is one of the most congested highways in the nation. It is regarded as a major cause for [urban decay](/wiki/Urban_decay "Urban decay") in the Bronx.[[102]](#cite_note-106)

The Northeast has been a place for many firsts in transportation in the US, from the first commercial railroad in the US in [Milton, Massachusetts](/wiki/Milton,_Massachusetts "Milton, Massachusetts") ([Granite Railway](/wiki/Granite_Railway "Granite Railway")), first rapid transit system ([MBTA Green Line](/wiki/Green_Line_(MBTA) "Green Line (MBTA)")),[[103]](#cite_note-107) the first limited access road was the [Bronx River Parkway](/wiki/Bronx_River_Parkway "Bronx River Parkway"), opened in 1922,[[104]](#cite_note-108) New York is also where the first urban freeway was built in the late-1930s.[[105]](#cite_note-109) ([FDR Drive](/wiki/FDR_Drive "FDR Drive")) The northeast would also be home to some of the first major [freeway revolts](/wiki/Highway_revolt "Highway revolt") in [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village"),[[106]](#cite_note-:1-110) and would see the first major highway teardown ([Miller Highway](/wiki/West_Side_Highway "West Side Highway")) in the 1970s.[[107]](#cite_note-111)

Before European settlement, most of the Northeast was loosely connected by Native American trails, some of which would be incorporated into early-European settlement roads and turnpikes. One major early road was the [Boston Post Road](/wiki/Boston_Post_Road "Boston Post Road"), connecting New York City and Boston along the Connecticut and Rhode Island coasts.[[108]](#cite_note-112) Later these roads would be included in the [King's Highway](/wiki/King%27s_Highway_(Charleston_to_Boston) "King's Highway (Charleston to Boston)"), spanning most of the east coast. Smaller turnpikes would also connect cities across the northeast. These roads would prove essential to moving goods across the English colonies in the 18th century and would later play a large part in the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution").[[109]](#cite_note-113)

The region saw a boom in canal-building in the early-19th century, with a major canal being the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal"), opened in 1825, connecting the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") to the Hudson River and Atlantic Ocean through [Western New York](/wiki/Western_New_York "Western New York").[[110]](#cite_note-114) The first railroads would be built in the late-1820s and would explode in mileage in the mid to late 19th century.[[111]](#cite_note-115) Places like Philadelphia, New York, Boston, Newark, and Pittsburgh would become large water and rail hubs during the Industrial Revolution and would see tremendous booms in population and use.[[112]](#cite_note-116)

Many large rivers in the northeast like the Hudson and Delaware would be slowly crossed with bridges starting in the 1800s, with the first fixed crossing of the Hudson River south of Albany being the [Poughkeepsie Railroad Bridge](/wiki/Walkway_over_the_Hudson "Walkway over the Hudson"), opened in 1889.[[113]](#cite_note-117) The [Delair Bridge](/wiki/Delair_Bridge "Delair Bridge"), which would connect Philadelphia with New Jersey was opened six years later in 1896.[[114]](#cite_note-118) The first crossing of the Hudson River into New York City would be the series of Hudson River PATH tunnels, being opened in 1908 and 1909.[[115]](#cite_note-119) The first major vehicle tunnel would be the Holland Tunnel, opened up in 1927.[[116]](#cite_note-120)

The start of highway construction would be the [Bronx River Parkway](/wiki/Bronx_River_Parkway "Bronx River Parkway") and [Long Island Motor Parkway](/wiki/Long_Island_Motor_Parkway "Long Island Motor Parkway"), both of which started construction in the early-1900s.[[117]](#cite_note-121) The rise of [Robert Moses](/wiki/Robert_Moses "Robert Moses") in New York would see the construction of many major road bridges and highways crossing the city and metro area. East River Drive (eventually renamed FDR Drive), was built along the corresponding river in Manhattan.[[118]](#cite_note-122) The mid-20th century would see the rise of urban and suburban [freeways](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway") and the decline of passenger and freight rail, with many lesser used tracks being abandoned or torn up during this time.[[119]](#cite_note-123) It would also see the original [Pennsylvania Station](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Station_(1910%E2%80%931963) "Pennsylvania Station (1910–1963)") demolished in Midtown Manhattan during the mid-1960s.[[120]](#cite_note-124) The construction of the [Cross-Bronx Expressway](/wiki/Cross_Bronx_Expressway "Cross Bronx Expressway") in New York, [Central Artery](/wiki/Central_Artery "Central Artery") in Boston, and the [Vine Street Expressway](/wiki/Interstate_676 "Interstate 676") in Philadelphia tore up many ethnic and minority neighborhoods in the name of [urban renewal](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal").[[121]](#cite_note-125)[[122]](#cite_note-126)[[123]](#cite_note-127)[[124]](#cite_note-:2-128) Many other highways were proposed during this era, like the [Lower Manhattan Expressway](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan_Expressway "Lower Manhattan Expressway") and the [Inner Belt](/wiki/Interstate_695_(Massachusetts) "Interstate 695 (Massachusetts)") in Boston, which were not built due to fierce highway revolts and rising costs.[[106]](#cite_note-:1-110)[[124]](#cite_note-:2-128)[[125]](#cite_note-129) After the major highway revolts and rise of environmental concerns, new highway and interstate projects were mostly cancelled or shortened in the Northeast by the 1990s.

Despite the lack of new major road projects in the Northeast, the region has still continued to grow in population, resulting in the rise of alternative forms of transport like [HOV lanes](/wiki/High-occupancy_vehicle_lane "High-occupancy vehicle lane") or [commuter rails](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail"). This has led to the Northeast having one of the highest transit usage percentages in North America, with the Long Island Railroad being the most used commuter rail in the continent.[[126]](#cite_note-130) One exception was the [Big Dig](/wiki/Big_Dig "Big Dig"), a major road project that would tear down the former elevated Central Artery (I-93) and instead [tunnel it](/wiki/O%27Neill_Tunnel "O'Neill Tunnel") (and widen). It would also construct a [new Charles River bridge](/wiki/Leonard_P._Zakim_Bunker_Hill_Memorial_Bridge "Leonard P. Zakim Bunker Hill Memorial Bridge") and the [Ted Williams Tunnel](/wiki/Ted_Williams_Tunnel "Ted Williams Tunnel") (I-90). This would end up becoming one of the costliest construction projects in the world, costing $21 billion adjusted to 2020 inflation.[[127]](#cite_note-131) The former highway's path would become the [Rose Kennedy Greenway](/wiki/Rose_Fitzgerald_Kennedy_Greenway "Rose Fitzgerald Kennedy Greenway"), a large public park. The [Sheridan Expressway](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_895 "New York State Route 895") (former I-895) was also rebuilt into a boulevard in the late-2010s.[[128]](#cite_note-132) [Rochester, New York](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York") has torn down the [Inner Loop](/wiki/Inner_Loop_(Rochester) "Inner Loop (Rochester)") due to low traffic and to reunify neighborhoods in downtown and to create developable space.[[129]](#cite_note-133)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_United_States&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Culture")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
